Hi, and welcome back to the celebrity branding blueprint. My name is Greg Rowlett, and once again, I am joined by Emmy Award-winning director, bestselling author, and the co-founder of the celebrity branding agency, Mr. Nick Nanton. And here in this video, we're going through stage one of the celebrity branding blueprint, which is all about finding your niche, right? And this is like a super sexy topic to start this program off with, isn't it? Yeah, it is, but you gotta understand this, or else again, you're gonna be out in the wilderness with nothing to eat. So the biggest mistake most people make is becoming a generalist, because they can probably help a lot of people with what they do. So for instance, if I'm a dentist, right? That's what I do, I fix teeth, right? Well, that's great, and it's great that you can help everyone from babies to children to adults. That's great, but it's hard for me to identify why I would refer a friend, or I would go to you over the other 10 million dentists, whatever it is, right? The fact of the matter is you've got to pick a niche. You gotta pick a small section of the marketplace that you can, number one, afford to reach. You can speak their language, and you can help them understand why you are the best choice, but not only have the only choice that they should be going to in order to get their dental work done. Now that's just a dentist example. Right, right, so let's talk about, one of the things that we like to talk about when we have a live audience in front of us is the fact that if there were five dentists, or five financial advisors, or five brain surgeons, for that matter in front of us, you can't, I don't know the difference between any of them, and I don't think you do really either just by looking at them, right? You have zero clue, and so one of the things that we think we do very well in our businesses is we think that we do a great job of letting people know what we're doing, what's going on. It's kind of the equivalent of, if you've ever had a significant other of a spouse say, you must think I can read your mind. Well, you must think your clients and prospects can read your mind, because they don't know what you know. They don't know the voluminous amounts of things you've studied on pediatric dentistry, or whatever it might be. And yes, looking at everyone, they essentially usually have the same diplomas, the same stuff on the walls. That's one of the reasons why media credibility works so well, by the way, because most people in traditional fields don't have that much media credentials, that many media credentials, I don't use the correct grammar there. But the fact of the matter is that that is the point. It's hard to tell one person from the other, and the other thing about becoming niched down, you become a specialist, right? And specialties, people are always willing to pay more money for a specialist than they are for a generalist. And if the further you niche down inside of a specialty, the less people there are who have expertise in it, and so the more money people will pay to fix the problem they have, because they truly need an expert. It can be anything from fixing a heart problem, the surgeon's gonna get paid the most amount of money, is the guy who's done 10,000 surgeries on that one vein or whatever in there, and you know he's got great success, and if that's your problem, there's only one guy you want, but he can even go down to radio-controlled cars. I mean, if you've got a guy who specializes in teaching children six to 10 how to raise radio-controlled cars, not with gas, but with battery, and two different types of controllers, I don't know, I'm just making stuff up. But then there's a desire in the marketplace, no one wonders why you'd go to that hobby shop over the other one, you're saying, hey, go to this guy, because this is what he's good at, oh, you've got a six-year-old, he'd love this, and this is the guy who can help you get there, or someone who helps in the marketplace with, like we said before, we have a client who teaches moms how to lose the baby fat. Very obvious who she can help, who's great for her, and a woman who's looking to lose weight, who's just had a baby, she might think that her trainer, who's super skinny, doesn't understand, she's 19 years old, never had a baby, how could she possibly help me lose all this weight? Now, one of the things I think is important to note that in many businesses, this knowledge base is typically the same stuff, but people don't think that you understand their scenario, and so one of the biggest reasons, besides being able to actually reach, affordably reach a niche, that's one of the reasons to pick a niche, but the second reason is so that people think you're speaking their language, they actually believe that you can help them, whether or not you can help everybody, and in many cases, I tell people, look, it doesn't mean you can't help other people, doesn't mean you shouldn't be in 37 different niches, but you're gonna have to talk to each one individually, like you understand their problem, like you've been digging through the trash and reading their mail, right? That's what it takes in order to get people to pay attention and to trust you, because everyone thinks they're different, their problem is different there, and it is within their bubble, their scenario, not minimizing that, but the same problem can be cured, the same problem that 20 different people have in different circumstances, from the person who's the expert, they know how to cure them all with likely very similar strategies, right? Yeah, I totally agree. Now, a couple things, we're gonna hit on a lot of the points that you just covered there, and it was an incredible overview. From the first video in this series, we talked about why we wanna be a celebrity in our marketplace, and the niches is really good for that, because you again wanna be the celebrity, but only to a small slice of the pie, right? I wanna be able to walk around the mall and not get hounded, but to my specific market, I wanna be their guy, the person that they look to, that they turn to, that they're like, there's no one else that can solve my problem, but Greg or Nick, or insert your name here, so talk a little bit about just being a celebrity to a certain marketplace, and it even kinda goes back to both of our days in the music industry. You have a death metal heavy band that plays Christian music, there's a certain number of fans that that appeals to, but again, they don't have to appeal to the pop crowd. They don't have to appeal to the crowd, and the fact of the matter is, the best way to explain this is to give you an example. So when we first started, Jack and I first started this business, and working in kinda direct marketing world, where we knew we could bring a different level of personal branding and media and stuff to that audience, because that's where we started, I went to this conference, and it was about 1,000 people, and I went with a couple people from Orlando, so they knew who I was, but no one else knew who I was at all. So I come back and Jack asked me how the conference was, and I said, good, but just a lot of people I was trying to meet, I mean, doing the typical networking thing, whatever, just trying to meet people, and try to find out how you might be able to help people, how they can help you. Well, Jack said, well, I've got a strategy for you, and what I want to do is we need to create a newsletter, and we need to start sending it out, so like a newsletter, what do you mean, like an e-newsletter, no, a real physical hard drive and news, on real paper, with a stamp on it. And one of the things that we'll talk even more about newsletters later, but paper newsletters, if you do not have a paper newsletter in your business right now, the correct type of paper, paper lizard, I was gonna say, I don't know what it's at, correct type of paper newsletter will grow your business bar none. I've never seen it not work when executed correctly, so I'm gonna get out my soap box in that for a minute. But, so we said let's create a physical newsletter, and I want you to put together a list of the 100 most influential people in that world, that are kinda at that conference. So we did that, we started creating a physical newsletter, started mailing it to them every month. When I got to the next convention six months later, people would be walking down the halls and they would be saying hello to me, they'd be wanting to tell me their side of my story, as again, Dan Kennedy, one of the most quotable guys ever, a true living legend says, he says, a good newsletter should be the equivalent of sitting down to have a good cup of coffee with a friend, that's what it should feel like when someone's reading it. So when I tell stories about my kids, or I tell stories about my life, or whatever it was, the fact of the matter is people don't really wanna know the technicalities you know, they wanna know you're a real person, how you help people, what you like to do, and you gotta tie it back to business lessons or whatever it is that you do to make it relevant. They don't wanna just read, you know, you want to check your cheese today, and it was awesome. Spent 50 bucks, and I got pizza and beer, whatever, they don't wanna hear that. But you can tie it back in, but when you do that and you tell those stories through that form of media, people read it and they start talking to you about it. I even had a guy come up to me, and he had something circled, said, hey, my business coach gave me this, and told me I need to talk to you about this. And so it was like a prospect who was heated up, ready to go, because I had sent this thing, his business coach said, oh, you need to go talk to Nick. The guy, based on my picture, and one of the other reasons we use pictures and personal brands and everything, based on my picture, he knew who I was walking down the hall, if I didn't have my picture on there, nobody would have known who I was still. And so he knew who I was, he came up to me, he talked to me, he wanted to meet with me, and so you know, that's how it works. But you gotta be able to, we niche down right there, to make sure these 100 people knew who we were, because that at the moment was what we could afford, and that was what was important to us, because we knew that they all had their own spheres of influences. Right, their own sphere of influence that could bring us more business. Right, so you niche just to 100 people. Right, yeah. And if you can get on the radar of those 100 people, you know, in your marketplace it might be different, maybe it's a thousand people, maybe it's 20 people, but you identified those 100 influential key people, and you made yourself the expert, the celebrity expert just to that core group. Absolutely, and one of the things I think that'll make this easier to fathom, because people will go, whoa, wait, 100 people, I need thousands of people, you don't even know how many people you need. One of the things you can do is you can break it down to a kind of a million dollar business formula. And this is a simple formula, it could be, you can make it 100 million, 10 million, whatever you want, but let's say a million dollars, right? In order to make a million dollars, or to gross a million dollars in revenue, you need a thousand people giving you a thousand dollars, 10,000 people giving you $100. Or 100 people giving you $10,000, now this is starting to get easier, right? And I think all of our zeros are working out there, but you get the point. We'll make sure that pyramid's in your action guide. I'm not the best manager here on this deal, but the point is, if you simply break it down, it makes it so much easier to accomplish that goal of making a million dollars or whatever, and you can see how few people you really need to give you amounts of money to get to where you need to go. I can tell you from my point of view, we have clients who have very successful businesses by charging a little bit to a lot of people, but charging a fair amount to a lot less people is a great business model that you can build a million dollar business very quickly without having to pull your hair out, run all over the place. I mean, I tell the story that the way I made our first million dollars in sales in this business was we wrote the book and we'll talk something about that, and I went out and I traveled around to groups that had, they were marketing groups, and they had 20, 30, the biggest one I think was 40 people, and I gave away a thousand books and got these people to know who I was, and they would hire me for stuff, but I literally traveled around and gave away a thousand books and to this small audience, so at least at this point, a thousand people knew who I was, but we did a, and they didn't all hire me, not most of them didn't hire me, but we did seven figures in business off of giving away those thousands of books, but sort of that 1,000 books and traveling around and making sure that that group of people knew who we were. Yeah, so I wanna talk about probably the objection that everyone listening right now is, well, I don't wanna limit myself just to remote control, six to 10 year old to do this, and that, and that, and one of the great stories I always talk to people about is Brian Tracy, one of our, someone that we work with a whole lot, one of our good friends in this business, he didn't start out doing leadership training, entrepreneurship training, sales training, goal training, this is a 30, 40 year career that he's got, he started out as a door-to-door salesman doing sales training, and then once that started to expand, he expanded to the next niche, and the next niche, and all of a sudden people he was talking to sales were like, well, I run my own business, can you help me with my business too? Well, hey, for sales, you need to set goals in order to set your sales goals, right? And so it expanded naturally over time, and the other thing, like in my music business, is I was, in a former life, I was selling information products to musicians, and I really targeted the singer-songwriter, but it didn't mean that jazz musicians, or rappers, or cover bands weren't finding my stuff, so let's talk about that objection of, well, Nick, I don't wanna limit myself just to X. I don't want you to limit yourself just to X in the long term, I want you to be able to help everyone you can possibly help. Our mission here at the agency is to help the most people. Help the most people. What does that mean? That means if you're great at what you do and you're helping people, we wanna find as many of you people as possible to help you spread your message and help more people. But the only way to do that effectively, and especially to grow at any rate that you'd like to grow at, you've got to start with niches. You've gotta start, number one, breaking it down so that you understand, so you can afford to reach your audience, everyone. You can afford to reach those people, because if you can afford to reach everybody, call me right now, I would be glad to take your marketing budget and help you spend it, right? But most of us just can't do that. I mean, all the people that I can help, I can't afford to reach them. Like we're buying a lot of media right now in magazines and newspapers and trade journals. It's awesome and it works great when you figure it out, but there's a long lag time between revenue going out and revenue coming in. I can't afford to reach everybody. I don't think there's any business on any level that can always afford to reach all the people you wanna reach. So the thing you've got, by niching it down, you make a pool of people you know you can afford to reach, whether that's buying a direct mail list for a local cosmetic dentist who specializes in pediatrics, he knows how to buy that list, or it's a finite list of people that you can afford to reach on your own dime, right? And then secondarily, you can then speak their language because if you don't speak their language, you're losing anyway. They don't think you can really help them as just another blah mail piece that came that they're gonna throw out, they're gonna put the A, the B, the C pile, the C pile's typically right in the trash right now. And that's where it's gonna go because you're not speaking my language. So by not niching, you're missing a huge opportunity to do the same exact thing you were gonna do anyway. But you get people to pay attention. And we all know in this day and age with cell phones and everything, MySpace, Facebook, all over everything. Like you just did a MySpace reference. That's pretty sweet. You can tell how long to do this now. So it's all there all the time. People are constantly distracted. And so you've gotta get their attention. Again, you've gotta make it seem like you've been digging through their trash, the stuff they threw out, they didn't want anyone to know, but you can solve that problem for them. Yeah, so let's talk about those, the ways to actually get into their head. So Dan Kennedy calls them dog whistles. In some of our presentations, we call them just different points of affinity that you might have. Maybe you went to, maybe you're in a small town and everybody went to the same high school. I joke around all the time that everyone in our office is a Florida Gator except for me. But that's a connection point. I'm speaking your language if I'm talking about the awful call at the end of the game. Military, if you're talking to military personnel, Marines talk to Marines with a certain code or different language than, you know, Navy Seals, fraternity brothers or whatever it is. But there's hundreds of these connection points that help you to understand the language and the lingo, right? So if I'm talking to people about, I'm talking to internet marketers about internet marketing, I need to know the acronyms, right? SEO and PPC and all these things. And if I'm not using that, it seems like I don't understand that marketplace. You don't understand their world. And so one of the things about that is, let's talk about trust for a second. Sure. Because one of the biggest factors why people will not do business with you today is because they lack trust. It's because they've been burned before by either someone in your industry or just too many people in general in life, whatever it is. So points of affinity, like points, build trust. So if you are a, let's say you're a 16 year old skateboarder, are you gonna trust a cop or you're gonna trust another 16 year old skateboarder? Or you trust maybe a 30 year old who used to skateboard. And that's kind of a dumb one. Like a Tony Hawk or something, right? Yeah, you have something in common. And people, there's all sorts of points of affinity, but the more that line up, whenever I speak, I try to tell my story and I try to throw out what I call personal hooks. These personal hooks are things that people might grab onto. I usually talk about that. I've got three kids. I usually talk to them before the Gator fan. I usually talk about the fact that I'm from the island of Barbados and I tell a funny story about that. And I literally had one guy come up to me after a presentation one time. He said, Nick, I don't know you very well, but I love Barbados when I visit it. I'm signing up. He signed up for 24,000 on a program. Just crazy just that one connection point. Probably wasn't that one, but that was something he remembered. And these points of interest, these hooks I like to throw out, I want it to be that everyone in the audience would have something to talk to me about afterwards. We're gonna be standing around. We have things in common. Because let's be honest, people who are gonna do business with you, if they think you're like-minded, they think they're like you and you're like them, you might actually understand them. So that's one of the things that this does and allows you to build those points of trust. The other thing about it is, it's just a simple trust thing I think is worth bringing up. If you tell people what to expect and then you deliver that, they trust you. So even if you've never seen me before, look, we did this very first module before this one and we said here's what to expect. We don't even know each other. If we go through these modules and we deliver what we told you we're gonna deliver, now we're gonna have trust. Because we told you what to expect and you expected it. We have some clients who are financial advisors and just as simply as saying, here's what's gonna happen when you come into the office. Here's who you're gonna meet, here's their picture, and here's what you're gonna walk out with. We're not making any decisions that day, you're gonna walk out with a clear roadmap. They didn't even do anything for the client. But the client now trusts them because you know what, that guy's a pretty good guy. He told me what he was gonna do and he did it. And so one of the coolest things about building trust is using your points of affinity, but then also just laying out a step by step system of deliverables, what to expect. And once you deliver on that, people trust you. Yeah, so let's kind of take that to the next step, which is now doing this in the story that you create for them because weight loss is a real easy example. So if I'm a personal trainer and I'm trying to get people to my personal training facility, if I can talk about my own struggles with weight loss, that's gonna be a great connection point because I know what it's like to be overweight. I know what it's like to go to the restaurant, have peer pressure and order the wrong foods. I know what it's like to go into a big box gym and be scared of all the muscle heads who are doing curls and stuff. And I'm intimidated, right? But if you don't have that story, well maybe you have that story from a client that you can talk from their perspective. But again, you're trying to create that emotional connection through story. And I think that that's one of the biggest parts of when you're selecting your niche is now you gotta find these stories that you can pull out. Just like you said, you have your Barbados story, you have your kid's story, you have your Florida Gator story. So in your action guide, I want you to start thinking about the stories that connect you to your marketplace where there's an emotional connection. I know you and JW just finished releasing story selling, another bestselling book, and it really goes through all these different story elements. But to me, like you gotta have these different stories that you can pull out, right? So if I'm talking to women who are overweight, I can pull out my women who are overweight story. If I'm talking to this group, I can pull out this story and just insert it into my media, right? Right, and one of the reasons why people never grow their business to the level they could grow it is because they keep losing the deal. They don't understand, I don't know, I went to work with these high school swimmers and they know that I train, I don't know, Olympic athletes on the luge, but I just couldn't get this deal and I don't know why. So because they don't think you're speaking their language. They don't think that you can really do it. And so the simplest thing by niching it down, and again, you can speak, you can be in 27 different niches, 100 different niches, whatever it is, but you've gotta learn to speak their language because that's really where one of the biggest points of pain is for them is they don't think anyone really understands them. And it's a great point by using your story or client stories, those are great. If you can show social proof that you've helped someone else or multiple people in this situation, that's what works best. When we are going out to market to attorneys, we use testimonials from other attorneys. We're gonna market to other financial advisors. We use testimonials from other financial advisors because I'm not a financial advisor, Jack did it for years and years, so that helps. But now that we have relevant, big name financial market, financial advisor in the marketplace, that helps people understand, okay, Nick can help me. So we market sometimes the same exact product, the same exact service we do in five different niches with different languages, with different names, and people, we get business because they go, oh, they really understand me. When I go to speak at a dental conference, I don't particularly talk about the fact that I'm an expert in marketing personal trainers. It's just not what I do. But I do, I wanna go to the personal trainer market, I don't tell them much about the dentist, and I can help them both. It's really the same strategies, but we speak a different language more in front of them. Yeah, so I wanted to talk about that. It was a great segue here, is having different front end marketing pieces for these different markets. So it's having that story about dentists that you can pull into a dental presentation, about trainers pulling into that. But you can also do this on your front end marketing, right? So if I wanna just talk to a specific segment of the market, I can create an ad that is just for that segment of the market. I might be selling them the same service that I'm selling another segment of the market, right? So one of the examples right now, we're running full page magazine ads and a lot of different niche publications, and a legal publication, and a real estate publication, and a financial publication. And really we're trying to help all of them to become best selling authors, right? But the way that we talk to advisors is different than how we talk to the lawyers, and it's different than how we talk to the real estate guys. So let's kinda talk about the fact that, again, you can have multiple niches, but you have to speak that language, right? You have to speak the language, and again, we wouldn't have, we not thought about niching down to these publications. We wouldn't even know where to start. I mean, we might have ended up blowing money in these mass publications. Again, like most people do where, I mean, The Wall Street Journal is a great publication, but it's very expensive to advertise in, and there's a lot of people who are reading that who never would hire me. They're just reading it for the stock picks, just whatever it is, they just aren't gonna hire me. So mass media is an amazing thing, and when used correctly, there's a lot of businesses that mass media works great for, but you typically can't start out affording just that mass media. We've got, everyone has the personal injury lawyers who use the mass media. They've got mortgage brokers, whatever it is, there are people who have worked for real estate agents, which is really well for it, but you typically cannot start out there, and so you've got to niche down, speak the language, and then that helps you determine where your dollars are best spent, because the whole point of celebrity branding is building a sustainable business that again, can create legacy dollars for you. So there are luck stories where someone went and worked in their garage, worked really hard, and it became this global brand, which is amazing, but I can't tell you how to do that over and over and over again. What I can tell you through this system is how to do what we've done over and over again for clients that works every time based on a proven set of methods that you can then grow as big as you want. Yeah, so let's kind of wrap up this first stage, because I think really you should understand the importance of a niche at this point, but I think to really just solidify it is now, how do I select it? Out of all this stuff that I can do, out of all these people I can help, how do I select that niche? I think it comes down to a couple points. Obviously there's got to be enough of them. Right, that's true. They have to be able and willing to spend money. They've got to have a big problem that you can solve. That's really where you start, but let's talk about how we can take those things and say, all right, I'm going after dentists, or I'm going after this. Sure, one of the things that we do when people come in and they pass for consulting dance, we sit down, and one of the first things I ask them is, tell me about your background, what led you here to today? I don't need to know what color your fifth grade dance partner was wearing or anything, but we go through the relevant pieces, and it's so funny that so many people come in and they've been successful and they go, well, I did all this, but now I want to do this. And they go somewhere totally different, which is okay. And we help people do that if that's what they really want to do. But the point is, typically when you hear someone's life story, their scenarios, the things they've been through, the businesses they've worked in, the failures they've had, it leads them to a very unique place that no one else is like them. And if you can pick up on those points, you can figure out pretty readily the type of people who you might resonate with, who they might go, oh wow. So for instance, if you're now a really successful financial advisor, you recruit other financial advisors, but you used to be a high school teacher, you were a coach, you were a high school coach. You took a team at the state championships. You realized, whatever it is, we start drawing these parallels and you start saying, okay, well, here's where you're gonna have the greatest amount of attendees with other high school coaches who have motivation to make more money because they can't, they hate not being able to have their wife stay home from work because they're gonna have babies or whatever it is. And so you then start building the story based on what are the big pivotal points in your story that you can get people to resonate with. That's where I typically start. And then again, it's like, well, what kind of people are hiring you right now? And so there might be a huge, well, like for us, we do really well in the dental niche and the financial advisor niche and the personal trainer niche, the attorney niche, we have lots of different niches, but one of the easiest things to do is look at who's actually out of all your clients, where's the biggest sector coming from? And that may be the one you wanna go after. It may not be, you gotta look at all the factors. You might have 99% of the market share because there's not that many, but that's one of the easiest places to start looking to. Yeah, I couldn't agree more. And if you're staying within your current marketplace, I would prefer say, who's the client or customer that you wanna clone? If I had about a hundred more nicks, what would that do to my business? Or if I got rid of all my nicks, what would that do to my business? And so there's a fun exercise that we do at live events, the Homer Simpson exercise, because once you now choose that I want a hundred more nicks in my marketplace, well, I need to draw out what Nick really looks like. And I think that that's tough for a lot of people to know that Nick is a 30-something, he's an attorney, he's an Emmy Award winner, and how do I take all those different pieces and now clone them, because now I can go buy a direct mail list. I can go out and find an events where they are. I can find trade journals where he is. I can buy Facebook ads with custom auditing, all this crazy stuff, but I have to pinpoint who you are in order to market to you. So that's a great exercise for you as a do right now is first go through all the steps that Nick just mentioned, find those parallels in the stories to find the person that you wanna connect with and see if they're a person you actually wanna do business with. Because I like doing business with people I like. Absolutely. Much easier. And your Homer Simpson story is funny because you tell people everything you know about Homer Simpson and you ask them who their favorite TV characters, they write it down and tell me everything you know about him and they literally usually have a list of like 15 to 20 things. They say, tell me about your ideal client, what do you know about them? Tell me about the guy that's paying you money, what do you know about him? Right, two or three things, it's typical what you know. And so there's services actually by the way that can help you break down the actual demographics, which are awesome. You got to typically have about 1,000 clients to really make it work well, but or people who've hired you in the past at least, and you can break down the similarities, but the fact that the point of the exercise is awesome in that you probably don't know what you need to know about your target market or the people who are actually paying you money right now, we often know more about TV character. Well, let's dig down and try to figure out what we can, one of the similarities and the best guy who hires me because then I can go again, buy a list or I can start speaking that language, I can pick that niche, I can go after those people because that's where I'm gonna find more business. Yeah, and that is where this entire system starts is in picking that niche, finding that niche, and you guys have a plethora like that, the SAT word for the day, of exercises in your action guide to help you break down and find this niche because without it, steps two through five aren't really gonna help you very much because you have to know who you're talking to, who are we gonna send this media to, who do we want to see the PR, who do we want to see our marketing pieces, and it all starts here with the niche. So go ahead, go through these exercises, get started in finding that niche, and then we're gonna come back in stage two and now help you take that niche and create your brand. Sounds good, we'll see you then. See you there.
